title: Chapter 6: The View from the Top
content: The ballroom at the Meridian is something else.

I've seen it before — on other people's Stories, in magazine spreads, in the background of celebrity selfies I used to scroll past during breaks at the restaurant. But seeing it in person is like the difference between watching porn and having sex, except I'm not sure which one is which anymore. Crystal chandeliers the size of small cars hang from ceilings tall enough to have their own weather. Neon pulses through the room in slow waves — rose gold, ice blue, violet — like the whole space is breathing. The bass thrums in my chest, a second heartbeat, steady and synthetic. Bodies everywhere. Designer everything. Teeth. So many teeth.

I'm wearing a gown the app picked. Black, backless, architectural — the kind of dress that costs more than my mom's car. My hair is slicked into something sharp and editorial. My makeup is a whole situation. I look expensive. I look like someone who belongs here.
The flashbulbs hit me the second I step out of the car.
"Chloe! Chloe, over here!"
"Chloe, who are you wearing?"
"Chloe! Chloe!"

My name is being screamed by strangers. Actual screaming, like I'm a boy band, like I'm the Beatles, like I'm something worth losing your mind over. I smile — the smile the algorithm prefers, 73% higher engagement than my natural one — and walk the press line like I've done it a thousand times.

A woman in a headset appears at my elbow. "Ms. Hart, right this way. VIP section." She guides me through the crowd like I'm royalty. People part. They actually part. I catch fragments as I pass — "—that's Chloe Hart—" "—the Mephisto girl—" "—content is insane right now—"
Eight days ago I was serving pho to these same people.

Well. Not these exact people. But people like them. People who ordered fourteen-dollar appetizers and didn't make eye contact. People who left four dollars on a sixty-dollar check because they "didn't realize" the tip wasn't included. People who talked about "authenticity" while eating fusion small plates in a restaurant designed to look like someone's grandmother's kitchen, if your grandmother's kitchen cost two million dollars.
Now I'm one of them. Sort of. For now.

The VIP section is a raised platform with its own bar and a privacy screen made of living moss. It's supposed to feel exclusive. It feels like a display case. I take the seat I'm directed to, accept a glass of champagne I won't drink, and settle into being observed.
And then I meet Sable.

She materializes the way only someone who's been famous long enough can — suddenly, without announcement, as if the room simply rearranged itself around her. Blonde. Not blonde the way I'm blonde when I have two hours and a colorist. Blonde the way the sun is blonde. Polished. Every edge buffed smooth. She's wearing white, because of course she is. Her skin is luminous, like she has a ring light embedded in her actual skeleton. She's been the #1 influencer for two years. She has forty-two million followers and a brand deal with every luxury house that matters.
"Chloe!" She opens her arms like we're old friends. "So nice to finally meet you! Your content is everything right now."

Her hug is perfume and Pilates and professionalism. She smells like a Sephora had a baby with a tax shelter. When she pulls back, she holds me at arm's length, fingers light on my shoulders, and beams.
"I mean it. That series you did? The morning routine one? Obsessed. I literally shared it to my Close Friends."

I've done a lot of things in eight days, but I haven't done a morning routine series. That was someone else. Some other creator. She didn't even check. Or maybe she did, and it doesn't matter. Maybe the specifics don't matter. Maybe all that matters is that she's standing here, performing goodwill for the photographers at the edge of the platform, and I'm the prop.
"Thank you," I say. "That means so much coming from you."
Which? I don't know. It's what you say. It's what the app would have me say.

Her eyes hold mine. Blue. Impossibly blue. Contacts, maybe, or just genetics refusing to be fair. And for a second — just a second — the warmth in them flickers. Like a candle in a draft. I see something behind the glow. Something calculating. She's looking at me the way you look at a chessboard — not at the pieces, but at the spaces between them. Three moves ahead. Always three moves ahead.
Then the warmth is back. The flicker is gone. Like it never happened.
"We should collaborate sometime," she says. "I've been looking for someone with your — energy."
The way she says "energy" makes it sound like a disease.
"I'd love that," I say.

She squeezes my hand once, twice — a little pump of affection, like she's priming a well — and floats away to her next conversation, her next photo op, her next move. I watch her go. She's perfect. Absolutely, impossibly perfect.
And there's something wrong with her.

I can't name it. It's like seeing a room where one thing is slightly off — a painting hung a quarter inch too low, a lampshade tilted just barely wrong — and you can't stop staring at it. She's beautiful and warm and generous and everything an influencer should be, and underneath all of it, there's a hollow. A space where something used to be. A smile that never quite reaches whatever's behind her eyes.
I shiver. The AC is aggressive, but that's not why.
The party swirls.

I talk to people. So many people. A music producer with a diamond earring who wants me in his next video. A fashion editor who tells me I'm "having a moment." A venture capitalist who keeps touching my arm and saying words like "ecosystem" and "synergy" and "monetizable authenticity." I smile and nod and say the right things. I take photos — so many photos. My face is starting to ache from the smile. I sign things. A menu. A woman's clutch. Someone's forearm, which, sure, okay, I guess that's a thing now.
At some point I end up in conversation with a tech billionaire whose name I should probably know. He's telling me about his new platform. It's going to "revolutionize" something. Aren't they all.
"You know what I love about your content?" he says, leaning in, his breath warm and vaguely oaky. "The vulnerability. The realness. You don't try to be perfect. You're just — you."
I open my mouth to respond and hear myself say: "That's so kind. I really believe that authenticity is the ultimate luxury."

I didn't think of that. Those aren't my words. They came out fully formed, like a text prediction, like autocomplete for a person. I've said this before. Not in this conversation — I've never met this man — but somewhere. In a video? A caption? I don't remember writing it. I don't remember deciding it was true.
The billionaire nods like I've said something profound. "Authenticity is the ultimate luxury," he repeats. "I'm going to remember that."

He's going to remember words the algorithm wrote for me. He's going to repeat them at dinner parties and board meetings and think they came from a person. From me. From someone with thoughts and feelings and a soul.

I excuse myself to the bathroom and stand in front of the mirror for a long time. The mirror in the bathroom says I look like a movie star. The camera on my phone says I look like someone else entirely. Two different faces. Two different people living in the same skin. I don't know which one is real. Maybe neither.
A server brings me a fresh champagne. I didn't ask for one. They just appear, like offerings to a minor deity.

She's young. Maybe nineteen, maybe twenty. Black. Tired in the specific way that service-tired looks — not sleepy, but worn, like a coin that's been through too many pockets. Her uniform is spotless. Her braids are neat. Her nails are long and painted lavender, chipped at the tips.
She hands me the glass and our eyes meet.

And I see myself. Eight days ago. The same exhaustion, the same invisibility, the same quiet calculation of how many hours until this shift ends. She's standing in a room full of people pretending she doesn't exist, serving drinks she can't afford, smiling at people who won't remember her face tomorrow. I know exactly what that feels like. I knew it eight days ago.

I almost say something. Something real. "I was you last week." "How long is your shift?" "Hang in there." Something — anything — that isn't performance. A rope thrown across the distance between us. A spark.
"Thanks," I say. "Love the nails."
She nods. Moves on.

The moment passes like a train I decided not to catch. My chest tightens. I wait for the feeling — the little ping of connection, the warmth of recognizing yourself in a stranger, the thing that used to make serving tolerable, the thing that made me human. It doesn't come. The space where it should be is flat. A line on a heart monitor that should be jagged and isn't.
I sip the champagne. The bubbles are sharp and cold and taste like nothing.
I leave at midnight.

The penthouse is immaculate. The cleaning service comes every morning while I'm asleep or shooting or existing in whatever way the schedule tells me to exist. The surfaces gleam. Everything is in its place. It looks like a furniture showroom. Like no one lives here. Which is accurate, I guess. I don't live here. I stay here. There's a difference.

I kick off the heels and stand at the window in bare feet. The city stretches out below me — millions of lights, millions of lives, all of them happening right now while I stand here in my furnished penthouse in my algorithm-selected gown with my forty-something-thousand new followers and my brand deals and my moment.
The city is beautiful. It's also meaningless. Just light. Just electricity. Just a lot of people trying to be seen.
I press my forehead against the glass. It's cold. The cold is real. The cold is the realest thing I've felt all night.
My phone buzzes. I almost don't check it. I check it.

**[MEPHISTO]**
**10M milestone: approaching.**
**Estimated time: 2 weeks.**
**Deduction preview: Grief.**

I read it three times. Grief. They're going to take grief. Not sadness — I already lost that, or most of it, at 5M. This is grief. The big one. The one that lives in your bones. The one that makes you human when someone you love is gone.
Two weeks.
I have two weeks left to mourn.
I set the phone down. My reflection stares back at me from the window — ghostly, transparent, overlaid on the city. I don't look like me. I don't know what me looks like anymore.
I sit on the edge of the bed. I open my photos app.
This is a mistake. I know it's a mistake. I do it anyway.

I scroll past the recent stuff — selfies, brand content, event photos, all of it bright and curated and empty. I scroll past the in-between stuff, the early contract days, the first glow-up pictures. I keep going. I scroll all the way down. Past everything. To the oldest photos on my phone.
There she is.

My grandmother. Má. Her real name is María, but she's been Má since before I was born, since before my mom was born, since before anyone can remember. In the photo, she's laughing. I'm laughing too. I'm maybe fifteen. We're on her porch in Queens, and it's summer, and she's holding a mango like it's a microphone, and she's telling some story that has me wheezing. Her whole face is the laugh. Her eyes are crinkled shut. Her mouth is wide open. She is the most alive person I have ever known.

I look at this photo and I try to feel it. The grief. I know it should be there. I know because I felt it before — the ache that sat in my chest for months after the funeral, the thing that made me cry in the shower and on the train and in the walk-in cooler at work where no one could see. It was awful. It was beautiful. It was the most real thing I had.

I reach for it now. I look at her face — the face I loved, the face I miss, the face that's been gone for two years — and I reach for the feeling the way you reach for a tooth that's been pulled. You know the space. Your tongue finds it instinctively. You probe the hole where something used to be. But nothing's there. Just the shape of absence.

I can feel the outline of the grief. The memory of the shape it used to fill. But the actual feeling — the pang, the sting, the wet heat behind the eyes that used to come without permission — it's not there. It's like a song I almost remember. Like a word on the tip of my tongue. Like watching myself feel something through frosted glass.
I wait to cry.
I don't cry.

I close the phone and set it on the nightstand. I lie back on the bed — the enormous, pristine, perfectly made bed — and stare at the ceiling. The penthouse is so quiet. The city shimmers outside. My chest is flat. My eyes are dry. Tomorrow I have three shoots and a brand meeting and a live stream and probably more conversations where I say words that aren't mine to people who see a version of me that doesn't exist.
I go to sleep without crying.

That scares me more than anything Mephisto has said. Because the terms of this contract were clear — I read them, I signed them, I knew what I was giving up, or I thought I did. But this — this quiet, this emptiness, this flat line where my heart used to be — nobody warned me about this part. Nobody said the deductions would feel like nothing. Nobody said the absence would feel like absence. Nobody said I wouldn't even notice what was missing until I reached for it and found nothing but the shape of the hole.
Two weeks until grief is gone. And after that — what? Joy? Hope? The ability to feel the sun on my face and mean it?

I should cry. I should be terrified. I should be pounding on walls and screaming and tearing this dress off and running. Instead I just lie here, dry-eyed, hollow-chested, and think: *I should probably hydrate before tomorrow's shoot.*
The app has already written my next line.
I close my eyes.
I don't dream.
